quotes.verbatim_quote: A substitution simply means replacing one chord with another.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Provides the module's foundational definition that all later substitution techniques build on.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Tritone substitution is the process of substituting one chord for another a tritone away.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the most useful harmonic substitution in jazz per O'Rourke.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
C7 has E as the 3rd and Bb as the 7th.

F#7 has E as the 7th and A# (which is the same as Bb) as the 3rd.

The guide tones have essentially swapped places between these two chords.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Gives the theoretical justification for tritone substitution via shared 3rd/7th guide tones.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Because the two chords have those 3rd and 7th as common tones, they can be substituted for
 one another.
quotes.why_load_bearing: States the underlying principle (shared guide tones) that makes tritone substitution work.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Tritone Substitution Rule - You can replace any chord with another whose root is a tritone
 away from the root of the original chord.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Codifies the canonical tritone substitution rule in O'Rourke's own pedagogical formulation.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Keeping things simple most of the time and just adding a substitution occasionally to brighten up the
arrangement is the approach I find works best – a good arrangement is all about subtlety!
quotes.why_load_bearing: Provides the taste/restraint rule that governs all of O'Rourke's substitution methods.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
G major and E minor are called relative key signatures to each other because they share the
 same key signature.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the relative-key relationship that underpins the I-to-vi substitution rule.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Rule: you can replace the I chord of a key with the vi chord of the key.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Codifies the relative-substitution rule for major-to-minor reharmonization.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Rule: you can substitute a major chord for chord iii of its key.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Gives the second major-to-minor substitution rule, completing the major-to-minor pair.

quotes.verbatim_quote: This process is referred to as chord addition – adding extra chords to the harmony.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Names the technique of inserting substitute chords as additions to the original harmony.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Pat Martino was a great proponent of what’s known as the "minor conversion concept" - a theory
 that any chord you see in a progression could be substituted for a minor chord instead.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Cites the Pat Martino concept that licenses minor-for-anything substitution in this method.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
You can use a minor 7 chord from the 5th of any dominant chord (which is like using the iim7 that
 belongs to the dominant chord).

 For example: an F7 chord’s 5th interval is C. So you could play Cm7 over F7 to get an interesting
 sound.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Codifies the minor-for-dominant substitution as a concrete rule with worked example.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
When one cycles anti-clockwise (backward) through the cycle of 5ths, we get a sequence of root
tones that are separated by the interval of a 4th.

This cycle of root notes a 4th apart is the most common root movement in Western music.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines back cycling and grounds it in the most common root movement in the genre.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Back cycling substitution rule - you can add chords to the chord progression of a song based
 on the ‘back cycle’, i.e. the circle of fourths.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Codifies the back-cycling reharmonization rule in canonical form.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Melodic common tone substitution rule: substitute any chord for another chord, as long as
 the new chord has a note in common with it that you use as the melody note on top.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Codifies O'Rourke's fifth substitution approach, the one that produces modern Rosenwinkel/Kreisberg-style colors.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Have the metronome click on every beat.
   Then try the metronome clicking on beats 1 and 3 only.
   Then have a go with the metronome on beats 2 and 4 only.

The final version is most desirable for jazz as it helps give the music more of a swing.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Gives the concrete metronome progression and identifies the 2-and-4 click as the jazz-preferred form.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Instead, methodically work through just 1 or 2 bars per session. Spend time slowly practicing the
fingering and carefully checking the notes are accurate and that everything is moving comfortably
under your fingers.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Specifies the per-session learning quantum O'Rourke prescribes for mastering an arrangement.
